volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Casina (Section 3.6.737c - Section 4.4.826b)

sections.label: Section 3.6.737c
sections.original: Omítte .
sections.translation: Let me alone .

sections.label: Section 3.6.738
sections.original: Servós sum tuós .
sections.translation: I am your slave .

sections.label: Section 3.6.738b
sections.original: Optumést .
sections.translation: That ’ s very good .

sections.label: Section 3.6.738c
sections.original: Opsecró te ,
sections.translation: My dear little Olympio , my father , my patron , I do beg of you —

sections.label: Section 3.6.739b
sections.original: Em ,
sections.translation: Well , you certainly are in your senses .

sections.label: Section 3.6.740b
sections.original: Tuos sum équidem .
sections.translation: Of course I am your slave .

sections.label: Section 3.6.741
sections.original: Quid mi ópust servo tam néquam ?
sections.translation: What need have I of so worthless a slave ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.742
sections.original: Quid núnc ? quam mox recreás me ?
sections.translation: Well now , how soon are you going to provide me some amusement [*] ?

sections.label: Section 3.6.743
sections.original: Cená modo si sit cócta .
sections.translation: If the dinner were but drest .

sections.label: Section 3.6.744
sections.original: Hisce érgo abeant .
sections.translation: Then let them be off this instant in-doors .

sections.label: Section 3.6.744b
sections.original: Properé cito intró ite ét cito deproperáte .
sections.translation: Go you into the house and despatch with all haste .

sections.label: Section 3.6.746
sections.original: ego iam íntus ero , facite cenam mihi ut ebria sit .
sections.translation: I ’ ll come in just now . Have the dinner charmingly sauced up [*] for me ; I want to have a charming meal . I really don ’ t care , now , to be eating in the style of your sumptuous foreigners [*] . Be off , will you ; but for the present , however , I take up my abode here .

sections.label: Section 3.6.749
sections.original: Gladiúm Casinam intus habére ait , qui me átque te intérimat .
sections.translation: The servant-maid says that Casina has got a sword in-doors , to deprive you and me of life with it .

sections.label: Section 3.6.750
sections.original: Scio . síc sine habére ;
sections.translation: I understand it . Just let her alone with it . They are imposing on you : I know these worthless baggages . However , do you now go into the house with me .

sections.label: Section 3.6.754b
sections.original: At pól malum métuo .
sections.translation: But , i ’ faith , I ’ m fearful of mischief :

sections.label: Section 3.6.755
sections.original: i tú modo , perspicitó prius quid íntus agátur .
sections.translation: only do you go . Reconnoitre , first , what ’ s going on within .

sections.label: Section 3.6.756
sections.original: Tam míhi mea víta
sections.translation: My life ’ s as dear to me as yours is to you .

sections.label: Section 3.6.758
sections.original: verum í modo .
sections.translation: But only do go now .

sections.label: Section 3.6.758b
sections.original: Si tú iubes , em íbitur tecum .
sections.translation: If you ’ ll go yourself , I ’ ll go in with you .

sections.label: Section 4.1.759
sections.original: Nec pol ego Nemeae credo neque ego Olympiae
sections.translation: Upon my faith , I do not believe that at Nemea [*] , nor do I think that at Olympia ,

sections.label: Section 4.1.760
sections.original: neque usquam ludos tam festivos fieri
sections.translation: or anywhere else , there ever where such funny games as these ridiculous games that are going on in-doors here with our old gentleman and our bailiff Olympio . In-doors , all over the house , all are in a bustle ; the old man is bawling away in the kitchen , and urging on the cooks .

sections.label: Section 4.1.765
sections.original: “ quin agitis hodie ? quin datis , si quid datis ?
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you go to work at once ? Why don ’ t you serve up , if you are going to serve up ? Make haste ; the dinner ought to have been cooked by this . And then this bailiff is strutting about with his chaplet [*] , clothed in white and finely rigged out . And then these women are dressing up the armour-bearer in the bedroom ,

sections.label: Section 4.1.770
sections.original: quem dent pro Casina nuptum nostro vilico .
sections.translation: to give him to be married to our bailiff in place of Casina ; but the artful baggages very cleverly conceal what the upshot of this [*] is really to be . Then too , in a manner quite worthy of them , the cooks are very cleverly doing their best to the end that the old gentleman mayn ’ t get his dinner . They are upsetting the pots , and putting out the fire with the water .

sections.label: Section 4.1.775
sections.original: illarum oratu faciunt ; illae autem senem
sections.translation: At the request of these ladies they are so doing ; they , too , are determined to bundle the old fellow dinnerless out of doors , that they by themselves may blow out their own stomachs . I know these female gluttons ; a merchant-ship [*] full of victuals they can devour . But the door is opening .

sections.label: Section 4.2.780
sections.original: Si sapitis , uxor , vos tamen cenabitis ,
sections.translation: If you are wise , wife , you ’ ll dine , after all , when the dinner ’ s cooked . I shall dine in the country , for I ’ m desirous to attend the new-made husband and the newly-made bride into the country ( I know the mischievous habits of persons ), that no one may carry her off . Do you people indulge your appetite .

sections.label: Section 4.2.785
sections.original: sed properate istum atque istam actutum emittere ,
sections.translation: But do make haste and send him and her out immediately , that at least we may get there in daylight . I shall be here tomorrow ; to-morrow , wife , I ’ ll be having a banquet still .

sections.label: Section 4.2.788
sections.original: Fit quod futurum dixi : incenatum senem
sections.translation: ’ Tis as I said it would be ; the women are packing the old fellow dinnerless out of doors .

sections.label: Section 4.2.789b
sections.original: Quid tu hic agis ?
sections.translation: What are you doing here ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.790
sections.original: Ego eo quo me ipsa misit .
sections.translation: I ’ m going whither she sent me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.790b
sections.original: Veron ?
sections.translation: Really ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.790c
sections.original: Serio .
sections.translation: Seriously .

sections.label: Section 4.2.791
sections.original: Quid hic speculare ?
sections.translation: What are you looking for here ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.791b
sections.original: Nil equidem speculor .
sections.translation: Really I ’ m looking for nothing at all .

sections.label: Section 4.2.791c
sections.original: Abi :
sections.translation: Be off ; you are loitering here ; the others are bustling about in-doors .

sections.label: Section 4.2.792b
sections.original: Eo .—
sections.translation: I ’ m off .

sections.label: Section 4.2.793
sections.original: Abi hinc sis ergo , pessumarum pessuma .
sections.translation: Be off , then , will you , away from here , you jade of jades . ( PARDALISCA goes into the house .) Is she gone then ? I may now say here anything I please .

sections.label: Section 4.2.795
sections.original: qui amat , tamen hercle , si esurit , nullum esurit .
sections.translation: He that ’ s in love , i ’ faith , even if he is hungry , isn ’ t hungry at all . But see , the bailiff , my associate [*] , companion , and husband-in-copartnership , is coming out of doors with wreath and torch .

sections.label: Section 4.3.798
sections.original: Áge tibicen , dum íllam educunt húc novam nuptám foras ,
sections.translation: Come , piper , while they are escorting the new-made bride out of doors , make the whole of this street resound with a sweet wedding-tune [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.3.800
sections.original: hymen hymenaee o hymen .
sections.translation: Io Hymen hymenaee ! Io Hymen !

sections.label: Section 4.3.801
sections.original: Quid agis , mea salus ?
sections.translation: How fare you , my preserver ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.801b
sections.original: Esurio hercle , atque adeo hau salubriter .
sections.translation: I ’ m very hungry , faith ; and , in fact , I ’ m not thirsty a little .

sections.label: Section 4.3.802
sections.original: At ego amó .
sections.translation: But I ’ m in love .

sections.label: Section 4.3.802b
sections.original: At ego hercle nihili facio . tibi amor pro cibost ,
sections.translation: Still , upon my faith , love , I shan ’ t be making any trial of you . For some time past my inside has been grumbling with emptiness .

sections.label: Section 4.3.804
sections.original: Nam quid illaéc nunc tam diu intus remorantur remeligines ?
sections.translation: But why is she now delaying so long in-doors , just as though on purpose ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.805
sections.original: quasi ob industriam , quanto ego plus propero , procedit minus .
sections.translation: The greater the haste I ’ m in , in so much the less is she .

sections.label: Section 4.3.806
sections.original: Quid si etiam suffundam hymenaeum , si qui citius prodeant ?
sections.translation: What if I were even to trill an hymeneal lay ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.807
sections.original: Censeo , et ego te adiutabo in nuptiis communibus .
sections.translation: I agree to that ; and I ’ ll help you at these our common nuptials .

sections.label: Section 4.3.808
sections.original: Hymen hymenaee o hymen .
sections.translation: Hymen hymenaee ! Io Hymen !

sections.label: Section 4.3.809
sections.original: Perii hercle ego miser , dirumpi hymenaeum cantando licet :
sections.translation: Upon my faith , I ’ m dreadfully done up ; one may burst one ’ s self with singing this hymeneal lay ; if I do burst this way [*] , I can ’ t burst any other , that I may make sure of .

sections.label: Section 4.3.811
sections.original: Edepol ne tu , sí equos esses , esses indomabilis .
sections.translation: Upon my faith , for sure , if you were a horse , you ’ d never be broken in .

sections.label: Section 4.3.812
sections.original: Quo argumento ?
sections.translation: On what grounds ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.812b
sections.original: Nimis tenax es .
sections.translation: You are too hard-mouthed .

sections.label: Section 4.3.812c
sections.original: Num me uspiam ?
sections.translation: Have you ever found me so ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.813
sections.original: Di melius faciant . sed crepuit ostium , exitur foras .
sections.translation: The Gods forbid ! But the door makes a noise ; they are coming out .

sections.label: Section 4.3.814
sections.original: Di hercle me cupiunt servatum . iam oboluit Casinus procul .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , the Gods do will me to be preserved at last . I already smell Casina [*] at a distance . ( They move to a distance .)

sections.label: Section 4.4.815
sections.original: Sensim supera tolle limen pedes , mea nova nupta ;
sections.translation: Move on , and raise your feet a little over the threshold [*] , newly-married bride ; prosperously commence this journey , that you may always be alive for your husband , that you may be his superior in power , and the conqueror , and that your rule may gain the upper hand . Let your husband find you in clothes ; you plunder your husband ;

sections.label: Section 4.4.820
sections.original: noctúque et diu ut viro súbdola sis ,
sections.translation: by night and day to be tricking your husband , prithee , do remember .

sections.label: Section 4.4.822_825
sections.original: Malo máxumo suo hercle ílico , ubi tántillum peccássit .
sections.translation: Upon my faith , at her downright peril , the instant she offends me ever so little !

sections.label: Section 4.4.826
sections.original: Tace .
sections.translation: Hold your tongue .

sections.label: Section 4.4.826b
sections.original: Nón taceo .
sections.translation: I shall not hold my tongue .
